coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots from wool for multicolor cell imaging, patterning, and biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lihong
Zhao, Bo
Li, Xiaofeng
Zhang, Guomei
Zhang, Yan
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: We report an eco-friendly strategy for fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) and demonstrate their applications for multicolor cell imaging, patterning, and biosensing. N-CDs have been synthesized using wool as raw material via pyrolysis and microwave treatment, providing a green way for the production of N-CDs without the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. The prepared N-CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield (22.5%), excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. Depending on these superior properties, the N-CDs have been applied in multicolor bioimaging, patterning, and biosensing. Imaging of living cells has been observed with high resolution using N-CDs as a probe, which validates their use in imaging applications and their multicolor property in the living cell system. Additionally, the obtained N-CDs have been used as fluorescent inks for drawing luminescent patterns, showing favorable application in anti-counterfeit and optoelectronic dimensions. Most strikingly, the as-prepared N-CDs could visualize Fe3+ fluctuations in living cells with negligible autofluorescence based on their high sensitivity and selectivity detection for Fe3+ ions with a linear range of 0.1–10μM and a detection limit of 10nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Multicolor cell imaging
Patterning
Biosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and PL enhancement of N-CDs
The synthesis and PL enhancement of N-CDs
The response of N-CDs to metal ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21306108 , 21575084 , and 21305082 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China ( 2013011009-5 and 2014011016-2 ), and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China ( 2014-018 and 2014-017 ), Innovative Talents in Higher School Support Plan ( 2014107 ), and Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar in Shanxi Province . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of N-CDs from pyrolysis and microwave irradiation of wool, along with photographs of the corresponding samples under UV light.
TEM images of N-CDs (A) scale bar is 50nm and (B) scale bar is 20nm. (C) Diameter distribution of N-CDs. (D) HRTEM image of N-CDs, scale bar: 2nm.
(A) Raman spectrum of N-CDs. (B) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. (C) C1s, (D) O1s and (E) N1s spectra of N-CDs. (F) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.
(A) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs. (B) Excitation dependent emission spectra of the N-CDs. Inset: corresponding normalized spectra.

(A) Fluorescence decay of N-CDs at 402nm (excitation at 340nm). (B) Effect of pH on the PL intensity of N-CDs (0.3mgmL−1). (C) Dependence of PL intensity on excitation time for N-CDs (1.2mgmL−1) under 340nm. (D) Effect of storage time on the PL intensity of N-CDs (1.2mgmL−1).
Cytotoxic effects of N-CDs on HeLa cells.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy image of HeLa cells labelled with the N-CDs (0.3mgmL−1) under 405nm excitation (A) and 488nm excitation (B). (C) The merged image of A and B. (D) bright field. (E) The merged image of A and D. (F) The merged image of B and D. (G) The merged image of E and F.
Different photographs of the patterns under UV light excitation (A–D), 470nm excitation (E–H) and white light (I–L).

(A) Selectivity of N-CDs solution (0.3mgmL−1) for Fe3+ over other ionic species under pH 7.0 condition at 340nm excitation. ([Mn+]=400μM). (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (0.01μM–400μM). (C) The relationship between F/F0 and Fe3+ from 0.01μM to 400μM. Inset: the good linearity in the range of 0.10–10.00μM.

Confocal fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells in the absence (A) and presence (B) of Fe3+ (400μM). Excitation wavelength was 405nm, and emission was collected in the blue channel (425–475nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of present method with some other eco-friendly methods for preparing CDs.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots from wool for multicolor cell imaging, patterning, and biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report an eco-friendly strategy for fabrication of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) and demonstrate their applications for multicolor cell imaging, patterning, and biosensing. N-CDs have been synthesized using wool as raw material via pyrolysis and microwave treatment, providing a green way for the production of N-CDs without the use of toxic/expensive solvents and starting materials. The prepared N-CDs exhibit exceptional advantages including high fluorescent quantum yield (22.5%), excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and satisfactory chemical stability. Depending on these superior properties, the N-CDs have been applied in multicolor bioimaging, patterning, and biosensing. Imaging of living cells has been observed with high resolution using N-CDs as a probe, which validates their use in imaging applications and their multicolor property in the living cell system. Additionally, the obtained N-CDs have been used as fluorescent inks for drawing luminescent patterns, showing favorable application in anti-counterfeit and optoelectronic dimensions. Most strikingly, the as-prepared N-CDs could visualize Fe3+ fluctuations in living cells with negligible autofluorescence based on their high sensitivity and selectivity detection for Fe3+ ions with a linear range of 0.1–10μM and a detection limit of 10nM.

ce:section.ce:para: Since their serendipitous discovery in 2004 [1] when researchers tried to purify carbon nanotubes by gel electrophoresis, carbon nanodots (CDs) have been magnificently transformed into nanolights. Typically, CDs are small discrete quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes less than 10nm, displaying size and excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. CDs offer a strong potential to replace traditional semiconductor quantum dots because of their superiority in unique luminescence performance, smaller size, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity. These particular characters endow CDs enormous potential applications, such as bioimaging [2], sensing [3–5], photocatalysis [6], fluorescent ink [7], mimetics peroxidase [8] and energy conversion devices [9]. Currently, CDs are typically synthesized by two types of strategies: top-down and bottom-up routes. Top-down approaches involve laser ablation or electrochemical oxidation of graphite [10,11], electrochemical treatment of multiwalled carbon nanotubes [12], and chemical oxidation of commercially activated carbon [13]. Bottom-up methods include pyrolysis [14,15], wet oxidation [16,17], hydrothermal synthesis [18–21], and microwave assisted synthesis [18,22] with all sorts of carbon precursors.

Generally, CDs consist of three common elements: carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. In recent years, doped CDs have been a subject of topical interest in carbon nanomaterial research. Particularly, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) have been synthesized because nitrogen atoms have a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons available for bonding with carbon atoms [23]. The nitrogen bonding to carbon may cause disorder in the carbon hexagonal rings and create new luminescent centers by trapping the radiative electron-hole pairs, so that N-CDs showed distinctive optoelectronic, electrocatalytic, and luminescent features from their N-free counterparts [23–25]. During the past few years, efforts have been put into searching for ascendant methods or materials to prepare N-CDs, such as ultrasonic treatment of glucose and aqueous ammonia [26], annealing of graphene oxide under NH3 [27], etc. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, all these above-mentioned methods suffer from these drawbacks such as time-consuming, intricate processes, high cost and severe synthetic conditions, which limit their wide applications. Recently, N-CDs have been prepared from the natural materials, such as grass [28], soy milk [29], strawberry juice [30], soya beans [31], and milk [32]. Unfortunately, N-CDs synthesized from these cheap and abundant precursors failed to achieve high quantum yield (QY) (usually<13%), causing difficulty for practical application. Therefore, the development of environmentally-friendly and cost-effective method for large scale synthesis of highly luminescent N-CDs remains challenging.

In this work, a green and economic strategy is developed for the synthesis of highly luminescent N-CDs with pyrolysis and microwave treatment using wool as a new carbon source. As-synthesized N-CDs show high fluorescent QY, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and satisfactory stability. Due to their favorable properties, the N-CDs have been successfully utilized to multicolor cell imaging, patterning, and biosensing.

AlCl3, BaCl2, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, HgCl2, KCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2, and ZnCl2 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing, China). NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Wool were form my domestic sheep (Shanxi, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity 18.2MΩcm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA). All the reagents were used as received without further purication. DDI water was used in experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and PL enhancement of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of wool. In a typical run, a crucible loaded with certain amounts of wool was transferred into a muffle furnace, and pyrolyzed at 300°C for 2h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black products were mechanically ground to fine powders. 0.5g of the resultant sample was dispersed in 40mL DDI water and magnetically stirred to form a black solution. The N-CDs aqueous solution was collected by removing larger particles through centrifugation at 4000rpm for 15min. Subsequently, N-CDs aqueous solution was subjected to dialysis for 12h. PL enhancement of N-CDs was achieved by further microwave treatment. Typically, the obtained N-CDs aqueous solution was treated in a domestic microwave oven (700W) for 6min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was carried out in a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Samples for TEM measurements were prepared by placing a drop of colloidal solution on carbon-coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The size distribution of N-CDs was performed by counting over 100 particles. Fluorescent photographs of N-CDs under UV light of 365nm were operated with ZF-2 ultraviolet analyzer for three purposes from Shanghai City Anting electronic instrument factory. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Bruker Tensor II spectrometer using a resolution of 4cm−1. The sample with 1mg diluted by KBr (ratio 1:200) was pressed into the disc. Raman spectroscopy was characterized using Bruker Senterra dispersive Raman microscopy with laser wavelengths at 532nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an AXIS ULTRA DLD electron spectrometer from Shimadzu Company using 300W Al Kα radiation. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (China). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

The relative fluorescence QY (Φ) of the N-CDs was calculated using the equation of Φx =Φstd Ix Astd η2 x/(Istd Ax η2 std). The optical densities were measured on Puxi TU-1901 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. In the equation, Ix and Istd are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs and the standard, respectively. Ax and Astd denote the optical densities (OD) of the N-CDs and the standard, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 was chosen as a standard with a QY of 0.54 at 360nm. ηx and ηstd denote the refractive index of the N-CDs and the standard, respectively. The absorbencies of all the samples in 1.0cm cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects.

For the cell cytotoxicity text, human cervical carcinoma (HeLa) cells were first plated on a Costar 96-well tissue-culture cluster and cultured at 37°C with 5% CO2 in air for 3h to adhere cells onto the surface. The well without cells and treatment with N-CDs was taken as a zero set. The medium was then changed with 100μL of fresh DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS containing N-CDs, and the cells were allowed to grow for another 24h. At least five parallel samples were performed in each group. Cells without treatment with N-CDs were taken as a control. After adding 20μL of 5.0mgmL−1 MTT reagent into every well, the cells were further incubated for 4h, followed by removing the culture medium with MTT, and then 150μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken for ca. 10min at room temperature. The OD of the mixture was measured at 490nm with a SunRisemicroplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grödig, Austria). The cell viability was estimated using the equation of Cell Viability (%) = (OD Treated/OD Control)×100%, where OD Control and OD Treated were obtained in the absence and presence of N-CDs, respectively.

HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. N-CDs aqueous solution (300μL, 1.2mgmL−1) was added to the culture medium (1000μL) at 0.3mgmL−1 final concentration. After incubation for 0.5h, HeLa cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed three times (1.0mL each) with pH 7.4 PBS (comprising 137mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl, 8.0mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0mM KH2PO4), and kept in PBS for optical imaging by an Olympus FV1000 confocal microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with 40×objective. Excited at 405nm and 488nm, blue emissions were collected using a 425–475nm band-pass filter and green emissions were collected using a 500–540nm band-pass filter.

N-CDs solution (1.2mgmL−1) is encapsulated into the pen for drawing versatile fluorescent patterns at room temperature. Commercially available papers (the paper showed no background UV fluorescence) were chosen as the patterning paper. Photographs were taken using a UV light and multispectral fluorescence vivo molecular imaging system (S-0010A). Blue fluorescence patterns were obtained using UV light excitation (excitation: 365nm; emission: 440nm). Green fluorescence patterns were obtained using appropriate filters for CD (excitation: 470nm; emission: 512nm long-pass filter; acquisition settings: 500–750nm in 10nm steps). Exposure times were automatically calculated and the acquisition setting was 1500ms.

The detection of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature in 10mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (containing 150mM NaCl, pH 7.0). In a typical run, 100μL of N-CDs dispersion (1.2mgmL−1) was added into 300μL of PBS, followed by the addition of Fe3+ standard with various concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 1min at room temperature. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate.

N-CDs-stained HeLa cells were treated with Fe3+ (400μM) in 2mL PBS (pH 7.0) for 1min. Fluorescence images were collected with an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser-scanning microscope. Excited at 405nm blue emissions were collected using a 425–475nm band-pass filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and PL enhancement of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The successful preparation of highly fluorescent N-CDs was carried out by using wool as the starting materials via pyrolysis and microwave treatment (Fig. 1 ). Wool contains protein mainly, which may serve as widespread, easily available, low-cost, and green natural carbon and nitrogen sources for large-scale fabrication of N-CDs. After pyrolysis of wool, obtained N-CDs show weak blue luminance. The PL enhancement is observed by further microwave treatment of obtained N-CDs. The rapid treatment using microwave irradiation could contribute to uniform size of N-CDs and further increase of the functional groups on the surface, resulting in the better PL performance [30].

TEM has been used extensively as a powerful tool to characterize the morphology and size. As shown in Fig. 2 A-B, N-CDs are spherical and well dispersed. The size distribution of N-CDs is relatively small and the majority falls within the range from 2.7nm to 9.3nm. Based on the statistical analysis of above 100 particles, the average diameter of N-CDs is 6.05±1.67nm. N-CDs show a crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.210nm (Fig. 2D) attributable to the (102) facet of graphite [33]. XRD pattern (Fig. S1) exhibits a typical broad peak at about 24°, associated with the graphitic structure [34].

The Raman spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3 A) exhibits two broad peaks at around 1382cm−1 and 1530cm−1, which are attributed to the D (sp3-hybridized) and G (sp2-hybridized) bands, respectively. The D band is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [31]. The intensity ratio of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (ID/IG) is 0.9865 for N-CDs, indicating that they have a structure similar to graphite.

The surface functional groups and element states of N-CDs were characterized by XPS. N-CDs are mainly composed of C, O and N element (the atom ratio of C:O:N is 4.74:1.72:1). As shown in Fig. 3B, N-CDs show three apparent peaks centered at 283.4eV, 398.9eV, and 530.0eV which can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3C) can be deconvoluted into four surface contents, that is, CC (284.8eV), CN (285.0eV), CO (286.2eV), and CO (288.2eV). In the O1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3D), two peaks at 531.5eV and 532.2eV are ascribed to CO and COC, respectively. The N1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3E) shows three peaks at 399.5eV, 401.1eV, and 401.6eV attributing to the CNC, N(C)3, and NH, respectively. The XPS results confirm that N-CDs are functioned with carboxylic and amino moieties.

We also used FTIR to identify the functional groups of N-CDs. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3F) exhibits characteristic absorption bands of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations of amine groups at 3430cm−1, C-H stretching vibrations at 2964cm−1 and 2928cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1636cm−1, CC stretching vibrations at 1573cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1136cm−1. In addition, the characteristic stretching band of the CN at 1406cm−1 indicates a successful introduction of nitrogen in CDs. The functional groups identified by the FTIR are in good agreement with XPS.

Fluorescence lifetime is the characteristic period that N-CDs remain in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state. Typically measured in nanoseconds, fluorescent lifetime is an intrinsic property of N-CDs depending on the nature of the fluorescent sites and the environment. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of N-CDs is assessed by fluorescent decay curves. As seen in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescent decay curve of N-CDs is recorded at 402nm under 340nm excitation. The decay curve is fitted using exponential functions Y(t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using the following equation: Y(t)= A 1exp(−t 1/τ 1)+ A 2exp(−t 2/τ 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ 1 and τ 2. We calculated the average lifetime of N-CDs to be 6.8491ns. The stability of the prepared N-CDs under different conditions was investigated. The PL intensity of N-CDs under pH values from 3.0 to 12.0 is measured (Fig. 5B). Interestingly, it is seen that the PL intensity of N-CDs keeps constant in the pH range from 4.0 to 10.0. Additionally, PL intensity of N-CDs remains almost constant even after excitation for 90min (Fig. 5C) and storage for 90day (Fig. 5D). The results indicate that the N-CDs have excellent stability, making them promising for meaningful applications and commercial purpose.

To explore the potential application of N-CDs in living cell, the cytotoxicity of N-CDs was evaluated by the MTT assay in HeLa cells. In the MTT assay, MTT could be reduced by the active cellular enzymes in the cells to insoluble blueviolet formazan crystals. The quantitative information about the cytotoxicity of N-CDs can be obtained by measuring the absorption on the cells. We performed the MTT assay in Hela cells with concentrations ranging from 0.1mgmL−1 to 0.8mgmL−1. The results demonstrate that more than 86% of cells are viable (Fig. 6 ), showing the low toxicity of N-CDs to cultured cells under the experimental conditions at a concentration of 0.3mgmL−1.

To demonstrate the potential application of N-CDs as a bioimaging probe, in vitro cellular uptake experiments in HeLa cells were performed. HeLa cells were labelled with the prepared N-CDs and the images were taken under a laser scanning confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 7 , after being labelled with N-CDs, it is seen that cells show bright blue PL when excited with a 405nm laser (Fig. 7A) and bright green PL when excited with a 488nm laser (Fig. 7B). The merged images with the bright field and the fluorescence images (Fig. 7E and F) display that the N-CDs can label both the cytoplasmic and the nucleus of HeLa cells. The newly developed CDs demonstrate their potential in cell labeling application or as multifunctional drug/gene carrier.

We applied the prepared N-CDs as fluorescent carbon inks for hand-drawn patterns. As shown in Fig. 8 , bright blue fluorescent patterns are observed under UV light excitation while bright green fluorescent patterns are observed under 470nm excitation comparing with the patterns under the white light. The “vis-invisible” properties of N-CDs benefit their applications in anti-counterfeit fields as well as optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of N-CDs to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system, the fluorescence spectra in the presence of representative metal ions were examined under pH 7.0 condition at 340nm excitation, including Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+, as shown in Fig. 9 A, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ show slight effect on the fluorescence spectrum of N-CDs while Al3+ and Cu2+ quench moderately the emission of 402nm. Fe3+ exhibits distinct quenchment for the emission of 402nm. This result reveals that the as-synthesized N-CDs are highly selective toward Fe3+ ions over the other metal ions and could be developed as an efficient fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ ions. The response of N-CDs for Fe3+ ions is attributed to a higher affinity between Fe3+ ions and carboxylic and amino moieties on the surface of the N-CDs, which leads to the quenching of the N-CDs fluorescence through electron or energy transfer [37]. The Fe3+ ions sensing capacity of as-prepared N-CDs was investigated in the range of 0.01μM to 400μM under pH 7.0 condition at 340nm excitation. Fig. 9B shows a gradual decrease in PL intensity at 402nm with increasing Fe3+ concentration, revealing that this system is sensitive to Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 9C shows the dependence of F/F0 on the concentrations of Fe3+, where F0 and F are N-CDs fluorescence intensities at 402nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The linear range of Fe3+ detection is 0.10-10.00μM and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.992. The detection limit is estimated to be 10nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

We used HeLa cells as a model to study the Fe3+-dependent fluorescence imaging of N-CDs using a confocal fluorescence microscope. HeLa cells were incubated with N-CDs (0.3mgmL−1) for 0.5h at 37°C, and then with Fe3+ (400μM) for 1min. As shown in Fig. 10 , N-CDs with blue fluorescence emissions disperse well within living cells. Furthermore, the N-CDs-stained HeLa cells exhibite strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 10A). After Fe3+ ions are added the fluorescence brightness decreases (Fig. 10B). Our results show that N-CDs can be used potentially to image Fe3+ in living cells under confocal fluorescence microscope. It provides a methodology to design CDs based fluorescent assays with high specificity for in vivo imaging and biosensing of metal ions and other species, which may be related to physiological and pathological events.

We have described environmentally friendly and low cost synthesis route to fabricate highly fluorescent N-CDs using wool as the precursor and demonstrated their applications in multicolor imaging, patterning, and biosensing, respectively. Due to their strong fluorescence and low cytotoxicity, N-CDs could be effectively used as bioimaging reagents. Moreover, these N-CDs have been used as fluorescent inks for drawing multicolor patterning. More significantly, easy cellular uptake behavior affirms the N-CDs as being promising for the detection of Fe3+ in living cell systems. Therefore, we demonstrate herein an extreme eco-friendly strategy to produce highly fluorescent N-CDs from widely available green carbon sources for versatile applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21306108, 21575084, and 21305082), Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China (2013011009-5 and 2014011016-2), and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2014-018 and 2014-017), Innovative Talents in Higher School Support Plan(2014107), and Technology Foundation for Selected Overseas Chinese Scholar in Shanxi Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.05.094.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controllable synthesis of green and blue fluorescent carbon nanodots for pH and Cu2+ sensing in living cells
Naked oats-derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging
A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on L-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
Green synthesis of carbon nanodots from cotton for multicolor imaging patterning, and sensing
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Facile and eco-friendly synthesis of green fluorescent carbon nanodots for applications in bioimaging, patterning and staining
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
Plant leaf-derived fluorescent carbon dots for sensing, patterning and coding
Facile synthesis of highly emissive carbon dots from pyrolysis of glycerol; gram scale production of carbon dots/mSiO2 for cell imaging and drug release
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
In vivo imaging of tumour bearing near-infrared fluorescence-emitting carbon nanodots derived from tire soot
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk-natural proteins
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Intrinsically fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission derived from hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of monopotassium phosphate
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Nitrogen-doped graphene as efficient metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction in fuel cells
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from glucose and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic ability
Exploration of the active center structure of nitrogen-doped graphene-based catalysts for oxygen reduction reaction

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
One-pot green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles as fluorescent probes for mercury ions
An absolutely green approach to fabricate carbon nanodots from soya bean grounds
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
Ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide solution: hydrothermal synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots as multifunctional nanosensors
Green preparation of fluorescent carbon dots from lychee seeds and their application for the selective detection of methylene blue and imaging in living cells
Green synthesis of stable and biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots from peanut shells for multicolor living cell imaging
Iron(III) selective molecular and supramolecular fluorescent probes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lihong Shi is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
Bo Zhao obtained B.Sc. degree in Chemistry from Shanxi Normal University in 2014. Now she is a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in analytical chemistry.
Xiaofeng Li received BSc degree in chemistry from Shanxi Nomal University in 1999. Now he is a postgraduate in Shanxi University. His research lied in supermolecular chemsitry.

Guomei Zhang received her BSc degree from the Shanxi University of China (Taiyuan) in 1997, her MSc degree and PhD degree in analytical and inorganic chemistry in 2003 and 2006 from Shanxi University, respectively. She joined the Chemistry Faculty of Shanxi University in 2006, and master tutor at Shanxi University since 2008 and has been a professor in 2014. Her current research interests include the DNA assembly and photoluminescence analysis of nanomaterials.

Yan Zhang received is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Shanxi University in 2008. Her current research interests include metal nanomaterials.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale,Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
